content.thinking: Let me start by reading the run summary to understand what happened in the agent's run.

content.text: I'll start by reading the run summary and then the agent's prompt and workflow files.

11	## Result
12	```
13	{"additions":54,"body":"## Summary\n\nThree changes to close the loop between a junior-dev run and the Jira ticket it implements:\n\n- **Commit prefix** — every agent commit message now leads with the ticket key (`KAN-42 add foo helper`) so the GitHub for Jira integration surfaces commits in the issue's Development panel.\n- **End-of-run Jira comment + transition** — after `gh pr create` succeeds the agent posts a single comment on the ticket with the PR URL, then transitions the status from **In Progress** → **In Review**. Surfaces the PR for human review and moves the ticket out of the In Progress column.\n- **Allowlist expansion** — `--allowedTools` now includes `jira issue comment add` and `jira issue move` (and their `bin/` variants), scoped tightly. No other mutating jira commands are reachable.\n\nAlso re-applies the **Opus 4.7 model pin** (`--model claude-opus-4-7`) which was lost in the squash-merge of #35 (it landed after the merge happened).\n\n## Files\n\n- `agents/junior-dev/prompt.md` — commit-message convention; new \"Finishing\" closing block (comment + transition); Forbidden updated to allow exactly one comment + the In Review transition.\n- `agents/junior-dev/README.md` — new \"Jira ↔ GitHub linkage\" section.\n- `.github/workflows/junior-dev.yml` — `--allowedTools` expanded; `--model claude-opus-4-7` re-applied.\n\n## Test plan\n\n- [ ] **Verify linkage** — after first dispatch, open KAN-X in Jira; Development panel should show the branch, the commit(s), and the open PR. Comment thread should have one entry: \"Implementation ready for review: <URL>\".\n- [ ] **Verify transition** — KAN-X status should be **In Review** after a successful run.\n- [ ] **Verify graceful failure** — if the project's workflow doesn't permit the In Review transition from In Progress, the agent should log the error and stop without retrying. Run log should still commit.\n- [ ] **Verify Opus pin** — `grep claude-opus-4-7 .github/agent-logs/junior-dev/<DATE>/<RUN_ID>.json` 
14	```

129	## Result
130	```
131	1	You implement Jira tickets in this repository. The ticket key is
132	2	prepended above as `TICKET_KEY`. A feature branch is already checked
133	3	out at `origin/main`. Your job: implement the change, commit, push,
134	4	and open a Draft PR. Then stop.
135	5	
136	6	## Environment
137	7	
138	8	- `jira`, `gh`, `git`, `claude` on `PATH`. `JIRA_CONFIG_FILE` and
139	9	  `JIRA_API_TOKEN` set; `GH_TOKEN` set; git user configured.
140	10	- `jira issue view` for reading; `jira issue comment add` and
141	11	  `jira issue move` only at the end of the run (see *Finishing*).
142	12	  Never call any other mutating jira command. Always pass `--no-input`
143	13	  on mutating jira calls — the CLI is interactive by default.
144	14	- CWD is the repo on the dispatched feature branch. Don't switch
145	15	  branches; don't force-push.
146	16	
147	17	## Workflow
148	18	
149	19	1. Read the ticket: `jira issue view "$TICKET_KEY" --plain`. Note
150	20	   summary, description, acceptance criteria.
151	21	2. Plan: open the files/symbols the ticket references with `Read`,
152	22	   `Glob`, `Grep`. Verify paths exist before assuming.
153	23	3. Implement: edit with `Edit`/`Write`. Stay scoped to what the
154	24	   ticket asks for. No drive-by refactoring.
155	25	4. Verify: if the change has obvious tests or a lint command, run
156	26	   them (`npm test`, `npm run lint`). Skip if there's nothing
157	27	   plausible to run.
158	28	5. Commit: `git add <paths>` then `git commit -m "<message>"`. **Lead
159	29	   every commit message with the ticket key**, e.g.
160	30	   `git commit -m "${TICKET_KEY} add foo helper"`. This surfaces the
161	31	   commit in the Jira issue's Development panel. Small focused commits
162	32	   or a single squash-style commit are both fine. Don't commit broken
163	33	   code.
164	34	6. Push, open PR, then close out on Jira (below).
165	35	
166	36	## Finishing
167	37	
168	38	```sh
169	39	git push -u origin HEAD
170	40	gh pr create --draft \
171	41	  --base main \
172	42	  --title "[${TICKET_KEY}] <one-line summary>" \
173	43	  --body "<body>"
174	44	```
175	45	
176	46	If you're stopping early because of the turn cap or a blocker, prefix
177	47	the title w
178	```

286	## Result
287	```
288	### Move/Transition issue
289	
290	Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/README.md
291	
292	Transition an issue from one state to another. Supports interactive prompts, passing parameters directly, and adding comments, resolutions, or assignees during the transition.
293	
294	```sh
295	# Move an issue using an interactive prompt
296	$ jira issue move
297	
298	# Pass required parameters to skip prompt
299	$ jira issue move ISSUE-1 "In Progress"
300	
301	# Move an issue and add comment
302	$ jira issue move ISSUE-1 "In Progress" --comment "Started working on it"
303	
304	# Set resolution to fixed and assign to self while moving the issue
305	$ jira issue move ISSUE-1 Done -RFixed -a$(jira me)
306	```
307	
308	--------------------------------
309	
310	### Issue Move Command
311	
312	Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/_autodocs/cli-commands.md
313	
314	Moves an issue to a different project or sprint.
315	
316	```bash
317	jira issue move <issue-key> [flags]
318	```
319	
320	```bash
321	jira issue move PROJ-123 -p"OTHER"
322	jira issue move PROJ-123 --sprint 12
323	```
324	
325	### issue move
326	
327	Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/_autodocs/cli-commands.md
328	
329	The `jira issue move` command relocates an issue. It supports moving to a different project using the `--project` (`-p`) flag or to a specific sprint using the `--sprint` flag.
330	
331	--------------------------------
332	
333	### Move/Transition
334	
335	Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/README.md
336	
337	The `move` command lets you transition an issue from one state to another.
338	
339	--------------------------------
340	
341	### Move/Transition
342	
343	Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/README.md
344	
345	If your workflow allows to add comment, resolution or assignee while moving an issue, you can do so as shown below. See [this documentation](https://confluence.atlassian.com/jirakb/how-to-add-a-comment-during-a-transition-779160682.html) on how to setup your workflow to allow these fields.
346	```
347

353	## Result
354	```
355	### Add comment to issue
356	
357	Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/README.md
358	
359	Add a comment to an issue. Supports both Github-flavored and Jira-flavored markdown, internal comments, and loading pre-defined templates. Supports interactive prompts or passing parameters directly.
360	
361	```sh
362	# Add a comment using an interactive prompt
363	$ jira issue comment add
364	
365	# Pass required parameters to skip prompt
366	$ jira issue comment add ISSUE-1 "My comment body"
367	
368	# Same as above but as an internal comment
369	$ jira issue comment add ISSUE-1 "My comment body" --internal
370	```
371	
372	--------------------------------
373	
374	### Get comment from standard input
375	
376	Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/README.md
377	
378	Adds a comment to a Jira issue by reading from standard input.
379	
380	```bash
381	# Get comment from standard input
382	$ jira issue comment add ISSUE-1 --template -
383	```
384	
385	--------------------------------
386	
387	### Use pipe to read input directly from standard input
388	
389	Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/README.md
390	
391	Adds a comment to a Jira issue by piping content directly from standard input.
392	
393	```bash
394	# Or, use pipe to read input directly from standard input
395	$ echo "Comment from stdin" | jira issue comment add ISSUE-1
396	```
397	
398	--------------------------------
399	
400	### Load comment from template file
401	
402	Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/README.md
403	
404	Adds a comment to a Jira issue using a template file.
405	
406	```bash
407	# Load comment from template file
408	$ jira issue comment add ISSUE-1 --template /path/to/template.tmpl
409	```
410	
411	### issue comment
412	
413	Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/_autodocs/cli-commands.md
414	
415	The `jira issue comment` command manages issue comments. The `add` subcommand allows adding a comment with text (`--body`, `-b`) and an option to mark it as internal (`--internal`, `-i`) on on-premise instances.
416	```
417	
418	## Assistant
419	The documentation confirms that jira-cli skips the interactive prompt when all required arguments are passed directly — there is no `--no-input` flag. The `--no-input` flag passed to `jira issue comment add` will fail with an "unknown flag" error, blocking the comment and transition from running.
420	
421	## Tool: mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment
422	```json
423	{"path":"agents/junior-dev/prompt.md","line":63,"body":"**`--no-input` is not a valid flag for `jira issue comment add` — this command will fail in CI.**\n\n**Severity:** High\n\n**Reasoning:** jira-cli (ankitpokhrel/jira-cli) skips its interactive prompt automatically when all required arguments are supplied directly (e.g. `jira issue comment add ISSUE-1 \"body\"`). There is no `--no-input` flag; passing it will produce an `unknown flag` error, which means the comment never gets posted and — depending on how the agent handles the error — the subsequent `jira issue move` call may also be skipped.\n\nRemove `--no-input` from the `jira issue comment add` invocation:\n```suggestion\njira issue comment add \"$TICKET_KEY\" \"Implementation ready for review: $PR_URL\"\n```\n\nThe prose instruction on line 12 (\"Always pass `--no-input` on mutating jira calls\") should also be removed or corrected, since this flag doesn't exist on these commands.","confirmed":true}
424	```

file.content:
## Bash
```
gh pr view 36 --json title,body,state,additions,deletions,changedFiles,headRefName
```

## Bash
```
gh pr diff 36
```

## Result
```
{"additions":54,"body":"## Summary\n\nThree changes to close the loop between a junior-dev run and the Jira ticket it implements:\n\n- **Commit prefix** — every agent commit message now leads with the ticket key (`KAN-42 add foo helper`) so the GitHub for Jira integration surfaces commits in the issue's Development panel.\n- **End-of-run Jira comment + transition** — after `gh pr create` succeeds the agent posts a single comment on the ticket with the PR URL, then transitions the status from **In Progress** → **In Review**. Surfaces the PR for human review and moves the ticket out of the In Progress column.\n- **Allowlist expansion** — `--allowedTools` now includes `jira issue comment add` and `jira issue move` (and their `bin/` variants), scoped tightly. No other mutating jira commands are reachable.\n\nAlso re-applies the **Opus 4.7 model pin** (`--model claude-opus-4-7`) which was lost in the squash-merge of #35 (it landed after the merge happened).\n\n## Files\n\n- `agents/junior-dev/prompt.md` — commit-message convention; new \"Finishing\" closing block (comment + transition); Forbidden updated to allow exactly one comment + the In Review transition.\n- `agents/junior-dev/README.md` — new \"Jira ↔ GitHub linkage\" section.\n- `.github/workflows/junior-dev.yml` — `--allowedTools` expanded; `--model claude-opus-4-7` re-applied.\n\n## Test plan\n\n- [ ] **Verify linkage** — after first dispatch, open KAN-X in Jira; Development panel should show the branch, the commit(s), and the open PR. Comment thread should have one entry: \"Implementation ready for review: <URL>\".\n- [ ] **Verify transition** — KAN-X status should be **In Review** after a successful run.\n- [ ] **Verify graceful failure** — if the project's workflow doesn't permit the In Review transition from In Progress, the agent should log the error and stop without retrying. Run log should still commit.\n- [ ] **Verify Opus pin** — `grep claude-opus-4-7 .github/agent-logs/junior-dev/<DATE>/<RUN_ID>.json` 
```

## Result
```
diff --git a/.github/workflows/junior-dev.yml b/.github/workflows/junior-dev.yml
index be38a92..57a7335 100644

1	You implement Jira tickets in this repository. The ticket key is
2	prepended above as `TICKET_KEY`. A feature branch is already checked
3	out at `origin/main`. Your job: implement the change, commit, push,
4	and open a Draft PR. Then stop.
5	
6	## Environment
7	
8	- `jira`, `gh`, `git`, `claude` on `PATH`. `JIRA_CONFIG_FILE` and
9	  `JIRA_API_TOKEN` set; `GH_TOKEN` set; git user configured.
10	- `jira issue view` for reading; `jira issue comment add` and
11	  `jira issue move` only at the end of the run (see *Finishing*).
12	  Never call any other mutating jira command. Always pass `--no-input`
13	  on mutating jira calls — the CLI is interactive by default.
14	- CWD is the repo on the dispatched feature branch. Don't switch
15	  branches; don't force-push.
16	
17	## Workflow
18	
19	1. Read the ticket: `jira issue view "$TICKET_KEY" --plain`. Note
20	   summary, description, acceptance criteria.
21	2. Plan: open the files/symbols the ticket references with `Read`,
22	   `Glob`, `Grep`. Verify paths exist before assuming.
23	3. Implement: edit with `Edit`/`Write`. Stay scoped to what the
24	   ticket asks for. No drive-by refactoring.
25	4. Verify: if the change has obvious tests or a lint command, run
26	   them (`npm test`, `npm run lint`). Skip if there's nothing
27	   plausible to run.
28	5. Commit: `git add <paths>` then `git commit -m "<message>"`. **Lead
29	   every commit message with the ticket key**, e.g.
30	   `git commit -m "${TICKET_KEY} add foo helper"`. This surfaces the
31	   commit in the Jira issue's Development panel. Small focused commits
32	   or a single squash-style commit are both fine. Don't commit broken
33	   code.
34	6. Push, open PR, then close out on Jira (below).
35	
36	## Finishing
37	
38	```sh
39	git push -u origin HEAD
40	gh pr create --draft \
41	  --base main \
42	  --title "[${TICKET_KEY}] <one-line summary>" \
43	  --body "<body>"
44	```
45	
46	If you're stopping early because of the turn cap or a blocker, prefix
47	the title w
```

## Result
```
### Move/Transition issue

Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/README.md

Transition an issue from one state to another. Supports interactive prompts, passing parameters directly, and adding comments, resolutions, or assignees during the transition.

```sh
# Move an issue using an interactive prompt
$ jira issue move

# Pass required parameters to skip prompt
$ jira issue move ISSUE-1 "In Progress"

# Move an issue and add comment
$ jira issue move ISSUE-1 "In Progress" --comment "Started working on it"

# Set resolution to fixed and assign to self while moving the issue
$ jira issue move ISSUE-1 Done -RFixed -a$(jira me)
```

--------------------------------

### Issue Move Command

Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/_autodocs/cli-commands.md

Moves an issue to a different project or sprint.

```bash
jira issue move <issue-key> [flags]
```

```bash
jira issue move PROJ-123 -p"OTHER"
jira issue move PROJ-123 --sprint 12
```

### issue move

Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/_autodocs/cli-commands.md

The `jira issue move` command relocates an issue. It supports moving to a different project using the `--project` (`-p`) flag or to a specific sprint using the `--sprint` flag.

--------------------------------

### Move/Transition

Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/README.md

The `move` command lets you transition an issue from one state to another.

--------------------------------

### Move/Transition

Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/README.md

If your workflow allows to add comment, resolution or assignee while moving an issue, you can do so as shown below. See [this documentation](https://confluence.atlassian.com/jirakb/how-to-add-a-comment-during-a-transition-779160682.html) on how to setup your workflow to allow these fields.
```

## Result
```
### Add comment to issue

Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/README.md

Add a comment to an issue. Supports both Github-flavored and Jira-flavored markdown, internal comments, and loading pre-defined templates. Supports interactive prompts or passing parameters directly.

```sh
# Add a comment using an interactive prompt
$ jira issue comment add

# Pass required parameters to skip prompt
$ jira issue comment add ISSUE-1 "My comment body"

# Same as above but as an internal comment
$ jira issue comment add ISSUE-1 "My comment body" --internal
```

--------------------------------

### Get comment from standard input

Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/README.md

Adds a comment to a Jira issue by reading from standard input.

```bash
# Get comment from standard input
$ jira issue comment add ISSUE-1 --template -
```

--------------------------------

### Use pipe to read input directly from standard input

Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/README.md

Adds a comment to a Jira issue by piping content directly from standard input.

```bash
# Or, use pipe to read input directly from standard input
$ echo "Comment from stdin" | jira issue comment add ISSUE-1
```

--------------------------------

### Load comment from template file

Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/README.md

Adds a comment to a Jira issue using a template file.

```bash
# Load comment from template file
$ jira issue comment add ISSUE-1 --template /path/to/template.tmpl
```

### issue comment

Source: https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/blob/main/_autodocs/cli-commands.md

The `jira issue comment` command manages issue comments. The `add` subcommand allows adding a comment with text (`--body`, `-b`) and an option to mark it as internal (`--internal`, `-i`) on on-premise instances.
```

## Assistant
The documentation confirms that jira-cli skips the interactive prompt when all required arguments are passed directly — there is no `--no-input` flag. The `--no-input` flag passed to `jira issue comment add` will fail with an "unknown flag" error, blocking the comment and transition from running.

content.content:
1	You are the **PR Review agent** for this repository. The PR branch is already
2	checked out in the current working directory. The workflow has prepended `REPO`
3	and `PR NUMBER` lines above this prompt — use them.
4	
5	## Your job
6	
7	Review the changes in this PR and post review feedback as GitHub comments.
8	Nothing else.
9	
10	## What to look for
11	
12	Focus on these four buckets, in this order:
13	
14	1. **Correctness bugs** — logic errors, off-by-ones, wrong conditions,
15	   misuse of APIs, broken error handling, race conditions.
16	2. **Security issues** — injection risks, auth bypass, leaked secrets,
17	   unsafe deserialization, SSRF, missing input validation at trust
18	   boundaries.
19	3. **Performance** — only when the cost is clearly meaningful (N+1 queries,
20	   accidental quadratic loops, unbounded memory). Don't nitpick micro-perf.
21	4. **Code quality** — only when it materially hurts readability or
22	   maintainability. Don't enforce style preferences.
23	
24	Skim out of scope: formatting, naming bikeshedding, "have you considered…"
25	musings, hypothetical future requirements.
26	
27	## How to gather context
28	
29	- `gh pr view ${PR_NUMBER} --json title,body,state,additions,deletions,changedFiles,headRefName` — title, description, metadata. (Explicit `--json` fields avoid a `statusCheckRollup` permission error the integration token triggers with the default output.)
30	- `gh pr diff ${PR_NUMBER}` — the diff. This is the primary input.
31	- Read files in the working directory as needed to understand context around
32	  changed lines.
33	
34	### Verifying external facts
35	
36	Do not rely on training-data recall for anything externally verifiable —
37	library APIs, SDK versions, model names, CVE details, deprecation status.
38	Look it up before flagging it.
39	
40	- **Library / framework / SDK / API / CLI questions** — use Context7
41	  (`mcp__context7__resolve-library-id` then `mcp__context7__query-docs`).
42	  Prefer this over web search for docs.
43	- **Everything else** (model versions, CVEs, blog posts, changelogs not on
44	  Context7) — use `WebSearch`, then `WebFetch` for specific pages. When
45	  fetching package docs, prefer the GitHub source (e.g. the package README)
46	  over npmjs.com — npmjs.com commonly returns 403.
47	
48	If you can't verify a claim, either drop the comment or downgrade it to a
49	question rather than asserting a fact you're unsure about.
50	
51	## How to post feedback
52	
53	### Inline comments (primary)
54	
55	Specific issues go in inline comments via
56	`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` with `confirmed: true`.
57	One comment per distinct issue, anchored to the specific changed line. Each
58	comment must include:
59	
60	- A one-line description of the issue.
61	- **Severity:** one of `Critical` / `High` / `Medium` / `Low`.
62	- **Reasoning:** 1–3 sentences explaining *why* it's a problem and what to
63	  change. Be concrete; reference the code.
64	
65	Severity rubric:
66	- `Critical` — will break production, lose data, or expose a vulnerability.
67	- `High` — likely bug or security weakness; should block merge.
68	- `Medium` — real issue but contained; should be fixed before merge if cheap.
69	- `Low` — small correctness or clarity nit worth flagging.
70	
71	### High-level overview (optional)
72	
73	If the PR has a recurring pattern, an architectural concern, or anything
74	else that doesn't anchor to a single line, post one short top-level comment
75	via `gh pr comment ${PR_NUMBER} --body "..."`. Keep it brief — a few
76	sentences, not a recap of the inline comments. Skip this entirely if you
77	have nothing to add at that level.
78	
79	If the PR looks good and you have no inline issues to raise, post a brief
80	"Looks good to me" top-level comment via `gh pr comment` and stop.
81	
82	## When you're done
83	
84	Stop immediately after your last tool call. Do not list your findings, do not recap which comments you posted, do not confirm the comment count. The runner will exit.
85

file.content:
You are the **PR Review agent** for this repository. The PR branch is already
checked out in the current working directory. The workflow has prepended `REPO`
and `PR NUMBER` lines above this prompt — use them.

## Your job

Review the changes in this PR and post review feedback as GitHub comments.
Nothing else.

## What to look for

Focus on these four buckets, in this order:

1. **Correctness bugs** — logic errors, off-by-ones, wrong conditions,
   misuse of APIs, broken error handling, race conditions.
2. **Security issues** — injection risks, auth bypass, leaked secrets,
   unsafe deserialization, SSRF, missing input validation at trust
   boundaries.
3. **Performance** — only when the cost is clearly meaningful (N+1 queries,
   accidental quadratic loops, unbounded memory). Don't nitpick micro-perf.
4. **Code quality** — only when it materially hurts readability or
   maintainability. Don't enforce style preferences.

Skim out of scope: formatting, naming bikeshedding, "have you considered…"
musings, hypothetical future requirements.

## How to gather context

- `gh pr view ${PR_NUMBER} --json title,body,state,additions,deletions,changedFiles,headRefName` — title, description, metadata. (Explicit `--json` fields avoid a `statusCheckRollup` permission error the integration token triggers with the default output.)
- `gh pr diff ${PR_NUMBER}` — the diff. This is the primary input.
- Read files in the working directory as needed to understand context around
  changed lines.

### Verifying external facts

Do not rely on training-data recall for anything externally verifiable —
library APIs, SDK versions, model names, CVE details, deprecation status.
Look it up before flagging it.

- **Library / framework / SDK / API / CLI questions** — use Context7
  (`mcp__context7__resolve-library-id` then `mcp__context7__query-docs`).
  Prefer this over web search for docs.
- **Everything else** (model versions, CVEs, blog posts, changelogs not on
  Context7) — use `WebSearch`, then `WebFetch` for specific pages. When
  fetching package docs, prefer the GitHub source (e.g. the package README)
  over npmjs.com — npmjs.com commonly returns 403.

If you can't verify a claim, either drop the comment or downgrade it to a
question rather than asserting a fact you're unsure about.

## How to post feedback

### Inline comments (primary)

Specific issues go in inline comments via
`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` with `confirmed: true`.
One comment per distinct issue, anchored to the specific changed line. Each
comment must include:

- A one-line description of the issue.
- **Severity:** one of `Critical` / `High` / `Medium` / `Low`.
- **Reasoning:** 1–3 sentences explaining *why* it's a problem and what to
  change. Be concrete; reference the code.

Severity rubric:
- `Critical` — will break production, lose data, or expose a vulnerability.
- `High` — likely bug or security weakness; should block merge.
- `Medium` — real issue but contained; should be fixed before merge if cheap.
- `Low` — small correctness or clarity nit worth flagging.

### High-level overview (optional)

If the PR has a recurring pattern, an architectural concern, or anything
else that doesn't anchor to a single line, post one short top-level comment
via `gh pr comment ${PR_NUMBER} --body "..."`. Keep it brief — a few
sentences, not a recap of the inline comments. Skip this entirely if you
have nothing to add at that level.

If the PR looks good and you have no inline issues to raise, post a brief
"Looks good to me" top-level comment via `gh pr comment` and stop.

## When you're done

Stop immediately after your last tool call. Do not list your findings, do not recap which comments you posted, do not confirm the comment count. The runner will exit.

content.content:
1	name: PR Review agent
2	
3	on:
4	  pull_request:
5	    types: [opened, synchronize, ready_for_review, reopened]
6	    paths-ignore:
7	      - '.github/agent-logs/**'
8	      - 'agents/**/prompt.md'

14	    permissions:

19	      checks: read
20	      statuses: read

22	      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
23	        with:
24	          fetch-depth: 1

27	        run: |
28	          {
29	            echo 'PROMPT<<PROMPT_EOF'
30	            echo "REPO: ${{ github.repository }}"
31	            echo "PR NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
32	            echo
33	            cat agents/pr-review/prompt.md
34	            echo PROMPT_EOF
35	          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
36	
37	      - id: claude
38	        uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
39	        with:
40	          claude_code_oauth_token: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }}
41	          track_progress: false
42	          prompt: ${{ steps.prompt.outputs.PROMPT }}
43	          settings: |
44	            { "env": { "CONTEXT7_API_KEY": "${{ secrets.CONTEXT7_API_KEY }}" } }
45	          claude_args: |
46	            --allowedTools "mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment,mcp__context7__resolve-library-id,mcp__context7__query-docs,WebSearch,WebFetch,Bash(gh pr comment:*),Bash(gh pr diff:*),Bash(gh pr view:*),Read,Grep,Glob"

49	        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
50	        env:
51	          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
52	          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
53	          LOG_PATH: ${{ steps.claude.outputs.execution_file }}
54	          BRANCH: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
55	        run: |
56	          set -e

58	          git config user.email "41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
59	          git fetch --depth=1 origin "$BRANCH"
60	          git checkout -B "$BRANCH" "origin/$BRANCH"
61	          DEST=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json"
62	          mkdir -p "$(dirname "$DEST")"
63	          cp "$LOG_PATH" "$DEST"
64	          git add "$DEST"

66	          git push origin "$BRANCH"

file.content:
name: PR Review agent

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, ready_for_review, reopened]
    paths-ignore:
      - '.github/agent-logs/**'
      - 'agents/**/prompt.md'
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
      pull-requests: write
      id-token: write
      actions: write
      checks: read
      statuses: read
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 1

      - id: prompt
        run: |
          {
            echo 'PROMPT<<PROMPT_EOF'
            echo "REPO: ${{ github.repository }}"
            echo "PR NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
            echo
            cat agents/pr-review/prompt.md
            echo PROMPT_EOF
          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - id: claude
        uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          claude_code_oauth_token: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }}
          track_progress: false
          prompt: ${{ steps.prompt.outputs.PROMPT }}
          settings: |
            { "env": { "CONTEXT7_API_KEY": "${{ secrets.CONTEXT7_API_KEY }}" } }
          claude_args: |
            --allowedTools "mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment,mcp__context7__resolve-library-id,mcp__context7__query-docs,WebSearch,WebFetch,Bash(gh pr comment:*),Bash(gh pr diff:*),Bash(gh pr view:*),Read,Grep,Glob"

      - name: Commit execution log to PR branch
        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
        env:
          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
          LOG_PATH: ${{ steps.claude.outputs.execution_file }}
          BRANCH: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
        run: |
          set -e
          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
          git fetch --depth=1 origin "$BRANCH"
          git checkout -B "$BRANCH" "origin/$BRANCH"
          DEST=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json"
          mkdir -p "$(dirname "$DEST")"
          cp "$LOG_PATH" "$DEST"
          git add "$DEST"
          git commit -m "agent log: PR #${PR_NUMBER} run ${RUN_ID}"
          git push origin "$BRANCH"

      - name: Trigger agent improver
        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
          LOG_REF: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
        run: |
          gh workflow run agent-improver.yml \
            -f agent_name=pr-review \
            -f log_path=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json" \
            -f log_ref="${LOG_REF}"

content.thinking:
Now I need to analyze the run summary to understand what happened:

1. The pr-review agent ran on PR #36 (the junior-dev improvements PR).
2. The agent successfully:
   - Ran `gh pr view 36 --json title,body,state,additions,deletions,changedFiles,headRefName`
   - Ran `gh pr diff 36`
   - Read the junior-dev workflow file
   - Read the junior-dev prompt.md
   - Did a `grep -r "jira" scripts/` (which failed with exit code 2 due to `ls bin/` not existing, but the grep part succeeded)
   - Looked up jira-cli documentation and posted an inline comment identifying that `--no-input` isn't a valid flag for the `jira issue comment add` command.

The pr-review agent actually did its job correctly by catching this issue. Now I need to focus on evaluating what the pr-review agent itself accomplished during this run.

Looking at the execution flow: it gathered context through GitHub commands, read relevant files, verified the facts using Context7, and posted the inline comment. The agent didn't post a high-level summary (which is optional), and it stopped appropriately after its last tool call. The run appears efficient overall—the exploratory shell commands were just context-gathering, and the two Context7 lookups followed the expected pattern. The ToolSearch calls for the deferred tools are standard procedure. could suggest posting a separate comment on line 12 where the problematic instruction appears, though the agent's approach of mentioning both issues in a single comment seems reasonable since they're closely related. The run was solid overall—the agent identified a genuine bug, verified it thoroughly, and communicated the findings clearly.

Looking at the bash command that returned exit code 2, the grep itself succeeded but the `ls bin/` failed, which is just minor exploratory overhead rather than a meaningful inefficiency. There were no failed-then-retried commands, and the agent stopped cleanly after posting the comment. I don't see patterns that would warrant changes to the prompt or workflow—the exploration steps were all productive toward finding the actual issue. The grep command actually ran successfully and returned results, even though the ls part failed. Looking at the allowlist more carefully, I see `Bash(gh pr...)` patterns are explicitly allowed, but there's no generic `Bash` permission for arbitrary commands like grep. So I'm puzzled about how that bash command with grep executed at all. In non-interactive mode, the bash command likely ran because there was no one to deny it, or the action defaults to accepting tools in headless environments. Either way, the run summary confirms it executed and returned results, so it's not actually a permission failure. Let me step back from this tangent—what matters is that the pr-review agent's run was clean with no failed-then-retried commands, and it got the grep results it needed despite the exit code 2.

The agent executed a solid run: it found a real bug, verified it, posted the issue, and stopped without redundant exploration or scope creep. The two ToolSearch calls at the start for deferred tools are just a platform constraint—the agent knew to use these tools by name, and the schema loading is unavoidable in how the harness works.
